fact: A blizzard trapped trains at Wellington, Washington for six days before the avalanche struck
source: The White Cascade: The Great Northern Railway Disaster and America's Deadliest Avalanche, Gary Krist (2007)

fact: The trains were parked on a narrow shelf cut into the mountainside, with a steep slope above and a gorge below
source: The White Cascade: The Great Northern Railway Disaster and America's Deadliest Avalanche, Gary Krist (2007)

fact: Rain fell on the sixth night, loosening the deep snowpack and triggering the avalanche
source: The White Cascade, Gary Krist (2007); also documented in Great Northern Railway historical records

fact: Passengers had begged to be allowed to hike out but the railroad refused, insisting the train was safer
source: The White Cascade: The Great Northern Railway Disaster and America's Deadliest Avalanche, Gary Krist (2007)

fact: Ninety-six people were killed, making it the deadliest avalanche in United States history
source: Widely documented; authoritative figure cited by the Washington State Department of Transportation and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration
